name: Calar de Cobos Loop
name_original: Puntal de la Misa por el collado de Góntar - circular gravel por el P. N. de las Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas
description: This difficult 30.7-mile circular gravel touring cycling route through P. N. de las Sierras de Cazorla offers stunning views and challenging
region_intro: Touring cycling around Siles offers a diverse landscape characterized by varied terrains, including undulating forelands and river valleys. The region features significant elevation changes, with routes traversing mountainous areas and offering views of natural features. Its topography provides a range of experiences for cyclists, from moderate paths to difficult mountain routes. Best touring cycling routes around Siles The most popular touring cycling route is Calar de Cobos Loop, a 30.7 miles (49.4 km) trail that takes 4 hours 52 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Siles – El Cubo Tower, Siles loop from Siles, a difficult 17.7 miles (28.5 km) path. This route includes a notable tower landmark and offers varied terrain. Local touring cyclists also love the Era del Fustal Shelter – La Piedra del Agujero loop from Siles, a 21.0 miles (33.9 km) trail leading through challenging terrain with over 1000 meters of ascent, often completed in about 3 hours 14 minutes. Touring cycling around Siles is defined by undulating forelands, river valleys, and mountainous areas. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate paths to difficult mountain routes. The routes in Siles are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.0 stars from over 2 reviews. More than 148 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Siles's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Siles?
A: There are over 20 touring cycling routes in Siles, offering a diverse range of experiences for cyclists. The komoot community has explored these routes over 148 times, contributing to an average rating of 4.0 stars.

Q: Are there any easy touring cycling routes for beginners in Siles?
A: While many routes in Siles feature significant elevation changes, there are options suitable for those seeking a moderate challenge. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40007196" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Siles Reservoir – Siles loop from Siles&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 20.7-mile (33.3 km) route that offers a less strenuous ride compared to the more difficult mountain trails.

Q: What are some challenging touring cycling routes in Siles?
A: Siles is known for its challenging terrain. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996251431/calar-de-cobos-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Calar de Cobos Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 30.7-mile (49.4 km) route with significant elevation gain. Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31508440" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Siles – El Cubo Tower, Siles loop from Siles&lt;/a&gt;, a 17.7-mile (28.5 km) path that includes a notable tower landmark and varied terrain.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in Siles?
A: Yes, many of the touring cycling routes in Siles are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996251431/calar-de-cobos-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Calar de Cobos Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31508433" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Refuel loop from Siles&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 20.7 miles (33.3 km) through challenging terrain.

Q: What kind of natural attractions can I see along the touring cycling routes in Siles?
A: Siles offers beautiful natural sights. You can visit the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2723905" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the Mundo River&lt;/a&gt;, a stunning waterfall, or explore the intriguing &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5974287" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Los Chorros Cave&lt;/a&gt;. Many routes also pass through scenic landscapes with opportunities for picnicking at spots like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4769649" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Charming picnic area&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any historical landmarks or castles to explore near the cycling routes?
A: Absolutely! The region around Siles is rich in history. You can encounter impressive structures such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5239771" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of Segura de la Sierra&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6536732" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Keep Tower of Hornos de Segura&lt;/a&gt;. Some routes, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31508440" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Siles – El Cubo Tower, Siles loop from Siles&lt;/a&gt;, even feature notable towers directly on the path.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about touring cycling in Siles?
A: The komoot community highly rates touring cycling in Siles, with an average score of 4.0 stars. Reviewers often praise the varied terrains, significant elevation changes, and the scenic river valleys that define the region's routes. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate paths to difficult mountain routes.

Q: Are there any long-distance touring cycling routes in Siles?
A: For those looking for longer rides, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e996251431/calar-de-cobos-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Calar de Cobos Loop&lt;/a&gt; is one of the longer options at 30.7 miles (49.4 km), typically taking around 4 hours and 52 minutes to complete. Other routes, while shorter, can be combined for extended touring experiences.

Q: Can I find places to refuel or rest along the cycling routes?
A: Yes, some routes are designed with rest stops in mind. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31508433" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Refuel loop from Siles&lt;/a&gt; is aptly named, suggesting opportunities to stop and replenish. Additionally, you might find facilities like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5524225" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Era del Fustal Shelter&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5713123" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;drinking water sources&lt;/a&gt; along various paths.

Q: Are there any routes that offer particularly scenic viewpoints?
A: Many routes in Siles traverse mountainous areas and river valleys, providing numerous scenic vistas. The challenging routes, in particular, often reward cyclists with panoramic views after significant climbs. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24524823" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Moheda Bridge – Yeste Castle loop from Cascada del Arroyo de los Marines&lt;/a&gt; offers beautiful scenery as it winds through the landscape.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Siles?
A: Siles offers varied terrain, and the best time to visit depends on your preference. Spring and autumn generally provide milder temperatures, which are ideal for tackling the region's significant elevation changes. Summers can be warm, while winters might present more challenging conditions, especially in higher elevations.
tips: The Góntar pass is the lower part of the Góntar hill (1691 m). It is located at the northern end of Calar del Cobo, where there is a huge vertically arranged opening or slit that characterizes this great rock formation.

This fountain located on the path comes to help us fill our water tanks and continue climbing towards the Collado de Góntar. It is a multi-level shed in which the cattle can drink as well as all of us who travel along this track. Someone has brought a hose from the spring itself here to quench our thirst.

The Prop of the Mass, with its 1,796 meters, is one of the most outstanding vertices of the Sierra de Segura, Jaen. From its privileged location, it offers us breathtaking and wide views in all directions, with the blue of the waters of the narrow Las Anchuricas reservoir that deliciously breaks the forest color of the black pine surroundings.

   The territory where it is located is integrated into the Cazorla, Segura y las Villas Natural Park, which is the largest protected area in Spain. It is also a Biosphere Reserve and classified as a Special Protection Area for Birds.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this gravel cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route primarily consists of gravel and forest tracks, with some paved sections. Be prepared for technical stretches and rocky landscapes, especially on the ascent to Puntal de la Misa (1,796 m). Some parts might even require you to push your bike due to their difficulty. The path alternates between wooded areas, shrubby zones, and more arid sections, occasionally crossing small ravines.

faqs.question: How difficult is this cycling route?
faqs.answer: This is a challenging route, rated as 'difficult' and requiring 'very good fitness'. It involves significant elevation gain and constant ups and downs, which can accumulate physical demand. Parts of the trail may even require 'hiker-biking' (pushing your bike) due to steepness or technical sections.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter several notable features. The Puntal de la Misa (1,796 m) offers some of the best natural viewpoints in the Natural Park. You'll also pass through the Collado de Góntar and can find water at the Cañada del Saucar natural spring. The route provides spectacular panoramic views, dramatic limestone walls, dense forests, and high-mountain 'calares' with historical agricultural terraces.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this route?
faqs.answer: While specific parking areas aren't detailed, the route is circular, suggesting various potential starting points. Look for designated parking within the P.N. de las Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas near access points to the trail, especially around the Góntar pass area, which is a key feature of the route.

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for families or beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is rated as 'difficult' and requires 'very good fitness' due to its significant elevation gain, technical sections, and demanding terrain. It is not recommended for beginners or families with young children.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Given the challenging nature and high-mountain sections, spring and autumn are generally the best times to cycle this route. Summers can be very hot, and winters may bring snow and ice, making conditions more difficult and potentially hazardous.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the trail?
faqs.answer: As the route is entirely within the P.N. de las Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas, general park regulations apply. Dogs are typically allowed but must be kept on a leash to protect wildlife and ensure the safety of other visitors. Always check the latest park rules before your visit.

faqs.question: Do I need any special permits or pay an entrance fee for the Natural Park?
faqs.answer: Generally, there is no entrance fee to access the P.N. de las Sierras de Cazorla, Segura y las Villas. However, certain activities or access to specific restricted areas might require permits. For standard cycling on established routes like this one, a specific permit is usually not needed, but it's always wise to check the official park website for any updates on regulations before your trip.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, portions of this route follow sections of the GR-247 "Bosques del Sur" long-distance trail, which is a well-known trekking and bikepacking route in the region. It also intersects with other official trails such as Variante 1 (1/2), Circular Prado Maguillo, and Cañada del Saucar - Puntal de la Misa.

faqs.question: What kind of views can I expect?
faqs.answer: Expect spectacular and diverse scenery. The route ascends to Puntal de la Misa (1,796 m), offering panoramic vistas that include the unmistakable silhouette of La Sagra. You'll see dramatic limestone walls, dense forests, and high-mountain 'calares' with pastures where local sheep graze.

faqs.question: Are there any water sources or facilities along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can find a natural spring at Cañada del Saucar natural spring. However, it's always recommended to carry sufficient water for the entire duration of this challenging route, as other reliable sources or facilities may be scarce.
